key_points: Heron's formula finds the area of any triangle given its three sides.
Can be applied to isosceles, scalene, and right-angled triangles.
Also used to calculate inradius r = Area/s and circumradius R = (a×b×c)/(4×Area).
Useful for triangles with decimal sides or when altitudes are not known.
examples: Triangle sides 13 cm, 14 cm, 15 cm → s = 21; Area = √[21×8×7×6] = √7056 = 84 cm²; r = 84/21 = 4 cm
Isosceles triangle sides 9, 9, 12 → s = 15; Area = √[15×6×6×3] ≈ 40.25 cm²; Altitude from base 12 → h = 2×40.25/12 ≈ 6.71 cm
Triangle with decimal sides 6.5, 7.2, 8.1 → s = 10.9; Area = √[10.9×4.4×3.7×2.8] ≈ 22.3 cm²
exam_tips: Check for isosceles or right-angled triangles to simplify calculations.
For decimals, carefully calculate (s-a)(s-b)(s-c) before taking the square root.
Use formula for altitude: h = 2×Area/base if needed.
Always compute semi-perimeter s first; it is critical for Heron's formula.
Double-check units for area, inradius, and circumradius.
